retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Indications -- FDA Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Radioactive iodine-refractory DTC Unresectable or advanced HCC Advanced RCC Endometrial cancer (in combination with pembrolizumab)

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The randomized, non-inferiority, phase III trial SELECT demonstrated lenvatinib use in HCC as non-inferior to sorafenib, a kinase inhibitor approved to treat HCC. The trial also demonstrated lenvatinib prolonged progression-free survival rate by 7.3 months and an objective response rate of 24% compared to sorafenib, 3.6 months and 9%, respectively. The SELECT phase III trial's efficacious results led to FDA approval for use as first-line therapy in unresectable or advanced HCC in patients not undergoing prior treatment. As per the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases, patients diagnosed with HCC and Child-Turcotte-Pugh A cirrhosis, for whom atezolizumab plus bevacizumab and durvalumab plus tremelimumab are inappropriate, should be offered sorafenib or lenvatinib as the primary treatment choices. A systematic review and meta-analysis examined the safety and efficacy of lenvatinib versus sorafenib for treating HCC within real-world scenarios. The study encompassed 9 single-arm investigations and 6 comparative studies. The results indicated that lenvatinib yielded notably extended overall and progression-free survival, with a higher objective response rate and disease control rate than sorafenib. However, lenvatinib exhibited a higher prevalence of hypertension than sorafenib, with no significant distinction in hand-foot syndrome. These findings suggest that lenvatinib may confer superior efficacy outcomes in treatment for HCC, although the safety profiles between the 2 drugs are comparable. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lenvatinib is a multiple receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor demonstrating potent antiangiogenic properties indicated as monotherapy or combination therapy for certain malignancies. Lenvatinib is FDA-approved for radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC), unresectable or advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), and advanced renal cell carcinoma (RCC).

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Administration -- Unresectable HCC
retrieved_docs.content: The suggested dose should be determined based on the patient's body weight: 12 mg orally daily for patients' weight exceeding or equal to 60 kg. 8 mg orally daily for patients weighing less than 60 kg.

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Older patients: In patients with DTC, no differences in safety or efficacy have been marked compared to younger subjects. Similarly, in patients with RCC treated with lenvatinib and pembrolizumab or everolimus, no significant variations in safety or efficacy are apparent between older and younger patients. A recent retrospective cohort study evaluated the efficacy and safety of lenvatinib in patients aged 80 years and older with unresectable HCC. Results showed similar outcomes between older and younger patients, with differing adverse event profiles. [15] [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Administration -- Unresectable HCC
retrieved_docs.content: Advanced endometrial carcinoma: 20 mg orally daily with pembrolizumab for patients with non-microsatellite instability-high or non-mismatch repair deficient (dMMR) progressive disease who are not candidates for X-ray therapy or curative surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Pembrolizumab -- Indications -- Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC)
retrieved_docs.content: HCC patients previously treated with sorafenib In patients with previously treated hepatocellular carcinoma, treatment with pembrolizumab resulted in an objective response rate of 18% at a data cutoff of February 2018. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lenvatinib is FDA-approved for the treatment of radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC), unresectable or advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), advanced renal cell carcinoma (RCC), and advanced endometrial carcinoma. The multiple receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor demonstrates potent antiangiogenic properties. The inhibition of the VEGF receptors prevents tumor angiogenesis, and the inhibition of FGFR, RET, PDGFRa, and KIT prevents further proliferation of malignant cells. The prescribing team should include primary care clinicians, oncologists, nurses, and pharmacists, crucial for early diagnosis, monitoring potential adverse effects, and optimizing dosage adjustments based on renal function, liver enzymes, body weight, and cardiac health. Routine monitoring for adverse effects like hypertension, cardiac impairment, QT interval prolongation, thyroid function changes, and reproductive considerations is necessary, along with patient education for better treatment outcomes and increased quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic impairment: As noted earlier, lenvaitnib is metabolized enzymatically in the liver by CYP450 isoform CYP3A and aldehyde oxidase. For patients with severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), dose adjustment is required. For DTC, the prescribed dosage is 14 mg orally daily. For RCC, the recommended dosage is 10 mg orally daily. Similarly, for endometrial carcinoma, the suggested dosage is 10 mg orally daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The Sorafenib Hepatocellular Carcinoma Assessment Randomized Protocol (SHARP) study, a multicenter, phase 3, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial with 602 patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma compared sorafenib (at a dose of 400 mg twice per day) to placebo and results revealed time to progression of 5.5 months versus 2.8 months and mOS of 10.7 months versus 7.9 months, in favor of sorafenib and statistically significant for both outcomes. Of note, the SHARP study only included hepatocellular carcinoma patients with Child-Pugh A, and a subgroup analysis showed HCV positive patients had a longer median survival of 14 months. Sorafenib was for long the only approved hepatocellular carcinoma option in the advanced disease setting until the Regorafenib After Sorafenib in Patients with Hepatocellular Carcinoma (RESORCE) study with regorafenib (a multikinase inhibitor) revealed positive results. This was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter phase 3, that enrolled 573 patients with Hepatocellular carcinoma whose disease has progressed after treatment with sorafenib greater than 400 mg/day and CP-A (97%). The trial compared 160 mg regorafenib once daily for 3 weeks on and 1 week off, or best supportive care plus placebo, with 28 days cycles. The results revealed a statistically significant median of survival of 10.6 months in the regorafenib group when compared with 7.8 months in the control arm. The Regorafenib After Sorafenib in Patients with Hepatocellular Carcinoma (RESORCE) study included 87% of patients with hepatocellular carcinoma and a Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer system stage C. Another option for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma whose disease progressed on sorafenib therapy is ramucirumab (a recombinant monoclonal antibody which inhibits vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2). In the REACH study, a median of survival was improved with ramucirumab (9.2 months) compared with placebo (7.6 months) but did not reach statistical significant (p = 0.1391). Nonetheless, a sub-analysis demonstrated a significant median of survival benefit for patients with an AFP level of 400 ng/mL or greater and Child-Pugh A for 7.8 months for ramucirumab compared with 4.2 months for placebo (HR, 0.67; 95% CI; 0.51, 0.90). REACH-2 study of ramucirumab in patients with advanced hepatocellular carcinoma with underlying Child-Pugh A cirrhosis and baseline AFP levels of 400 ng/mL or more is currently recruiting to evaluate this hypothesis. Ongoing clinical trials and future therapies are focused on immunotherapy (nivolumab and durvalumab) and small molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitors (cabozantinib). [15] [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Chemotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Sorafenib is the first-line treatment for the patient with BCLC advanced stage (C) with preserved liver function, ECOG-PS score of 1-2, and macrovascular invasion or extrahepatic spread. Sorafenib is a multikinase inhibitor. Sorafenib hepatocellular carcinoma assessment randomized protocol (SHARP) trial demonstrated a median survival of 10.7 months for patients receiving sorafenib as compared to 7.9 months in the placebo group. [57] In addition, sorafenib was shown to be effective in the Asia-Pacific region with patients having advanced HCC. [58] Common side effects of sorafenib include palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, diarrhea, weight loss, and hypertension. Lenvatinib was demonstrated noninferiority, but not superior to sorafenib. [59] Lenvatinib has been approved as the second agent for the first-line treatment of advanced HCC by the food and drug authority (FDA). Lenvatinib is associated with significant weight loss but less palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia when compared to sorafenib. Patients who are intolerant or have tumor progression on sorafenib are started on the second line of treatment. Regorafenib, which is a multikinase inhibitor, has been approved by the FDA as the second-line of treatment for advanced HCC. [60] Other second-line medications include cabozantinib, ramucirumab, and nivolumab, which is a programmed cell death 1 (PD-1) immune checkpoint inhibitor. [61] [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Chemotherapy has served as a treatment tool before and following surgical resection. Due to the limited incidence of FL-HCC, no RCT has elucidated the most efficacious chemotherapeutic regimen. Of note is that no neoadjuvant/adjuvant systemic therapies have yet been reported, showing an improved survival benefit in patients with resectable FL-HCC. [15] Chemotherapy with agents such as gemcitabine, cisplatin, 5-fluorouracil, interferon, and oxaliplatin have to be utilized and have shown varying degrees of response. [18] [19] Combined treatment strategies, including surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation, have shown better outcomes. [20] Decreasing the mass size before surgical resection with percutaneous radioembolization has been studied. [21] A novel targeted therapy that has demonstrated efficacy in treating HCC, sorafenib, was evaluated retrospectively in FL-HCC, showing limited effectiveness. [19] Potential targets in the mTOR pathway and/or Aurora A kinase were recently identified. Currently, no controlled trials have evaluated these targets. There has been a favorable outcome in utilizing rapamycin (mTOR inhibitor) in an unresectable patient. [22] Checkpoint inhibition has shown reasonable efficacy in a Phase II study of advanced HCC. [23] Controlled trials evaluating checkpoint inhibitors in FL-HCC are lacking, and case reports to date are limited and unequivocal. [15] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma patients with advanced disease is single-agent multikinase inhibitor which is sorafenib and no role for chemotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2020
retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and midostaurin are both classified as protein kinase inhibitors within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system under the category “Other protein kinase inhibitors.” This classification indicates that each drug belongs to the same therapeutic group of agents that target protein kinases, reflecting shared mechanisms of action and relevance in the treatment of diseases such as cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1290
retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and gilteritinib are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group designated “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” indicating that each drug belongs to this specific category of protein kinase–targeting agents. This classification reflects their shared mechanism of action as protein kinase inhibitors within the broader ATC system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” comprises the kinase‑targeted agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin. Both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated as potential therapies for acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). As members of the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, these drugs share a common mechanism of action that involves inhibition of specific signaling pathways driving malignant cell proliferation, and their use in AML highlights the therapeutic relevance of kinase inhibition in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: of these compounds enables clinicians to perform comprehensive risk assessments, optimize dosing, implement safety measures, and conduct vigilant monitoring to minimize harm while achieving therapeutic objectives.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, comprise dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically recognized for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Collectively, these agents illustrate how distinct kinase‑targeted drugs are organized within broader therapeutic families and linked to the management of various hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 83
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Within this subclass, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are recognized members, and ibrutinib has therapeutic activity against chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Another grouping, designated as other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC, also belongs to the broader class of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin are members of this ATC group, and both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Together, these kinase‑targeting drugs illustrate the role of protein kinase inhibition in the management of hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia.

Together, these entities illustrate a comprehensive therapeutic landscape that integrates hierarchical classification, mechanistic specificity, drug‑interaction considerations, diagnostic imaging agents, and a spectrum of antiviral modalities—from nucleoside analogues and entry inhibitors to monoclonal antibodies—each with defined indications and roles essential for optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 96
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a comprehensive pharmacologic landscape for blood cancers (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم), particularly leukemias such as lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are classified as protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity; the first three are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, also belong to this class, with ibrutinib specifically used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
